message.content:
You are confirmer #1, independent. Confirm this fix is BOTH appropriate (correct, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (the named test DISCRIMINATES — fails if reverted). Read the actual changed file + test at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.
Fix J-001 in scripts/capture_transcripts.py
What: scrubber FAILS CLOSED: decode errors='replace' (always scrub text), classify binary by a NUL byte not UTF-8 validity — fixes the CV-O-1 strict-decode fail-open re-leak.
Test(s): test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes, test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8
Verdict CONFIRMED or CONCERN; be specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

137	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
138	    try:
139	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
140	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
141	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
142	        # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
143	        # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
144	        #
145	        # FAIL CLOSED on redaction (#J-001): classify binary by an actual binary SIGNAL (a NUL
146	        # byte), NOT by UTF-8 validity. A transcript routinely embeds arbitrary subprocess stdout
147	        # (latin-1 dumps, truncated multibyte) inside JSONL strings; gating on strict decode meant
148	        # a single stray byte routed the WHOLE file to the verbatim branch and re-leaked it (the
149	        # CV-O-1 fix had turned an always-redact path into a fail-open one). Decode with
150	        # errors="replace" so redaction ALWAYS runs on text; only a genuinely binary blob (NUL
151	        # present) is copied verbatim.
152	        if b"\x00" in raw:
153	            data = raw  # genuinely binary — nothing to scrub
154	        else:
155	            data = _scrub(raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")).encode("utf-8")
156	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
157	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
158	        else:
159	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
160	        return {
161	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
162	            "bytes": len(data),
163	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
164	        }
165	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
166	        return None
167

126	def test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes():
127	    # #J-001: a stray non-UTF-8 byte must NOT disable redaction for the whole file. The scrubber
128	    # decodes errors='replace' and classifies binary by a NUL byte, so a secret next to an invalid
129	    # byte is still scrubbed (the pass-6 strict-decode fix had made this fail OPEN).
130	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_failclosed")
131	    raw = b'{"out":"[REDACTED] caf\xe9 /Users/[REDACTED]/x"}'  # invalid \xe9
132	    text = raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
133	    out = cap._scrub(text)
134	    assert "[REDACTED]" not in out  # secret scrubbed despite the bad byte
135	    assert "somebody" not in out
136	
137

141	    src = tmp_path / "t.jsonl"
142	    src.write_bytes(b'{"k":"[REDACTED] \x80 end"}')  # invalid \x80
143	    dst = tmp_path / "out" / "t.jsonl"
144	    cap._copy_scrubbed(src, dst)
145	    out = dst.read_bytes()
146	    assert b"abcdefghijklmnopqrstuv" not in out and b"[REDACTED]" in out
147

152	    monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "Ada Lovelace")

154	    out = cap._scrub("Author: Ada Lovelace <[REDACTED]>; also Ada   Lovelace in prose")
155	    assert "Ada Lovelace" not in out and "Ada   Lovelace" not in out  # flexible whitespace too
156	    assert "[REDACTED]" not in out  # email still scrubbed
157	
158

161	    # into production. It derives a fresh random secret per run instead.

content.text: Let me check the `_scrub` function to confirm it redacts these patterns, and the `_load` helper.

content.content:
112	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
113	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
114	        if pat.groups == 2:
115	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
116	        elif pat.groups == 1:
117	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
118	        else:
119	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
120	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
121	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
122	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
123	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
124	    # Redact the operator's full display name too (#J-002) — distinct PII the username/email rules
125	    # leave in cleartext (git author strings, pyproject authors=, prose).
126	    for pat in _DISPLAY_NAME_RES:
127	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
128	    return text
129	
130

content.text:
Confirmed discrimination. The end-to-end test `test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8` FAILS when reverted to the fail-open strict-decode version (the secret is copied raw), and PASSES with the fix. File restored to HEAD (grep shows 2 occurrences of `errors="replace"`).

Note: the first test (`test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes`) passes in both states because it calls `_scrub` directly on pre-decoded text — it does NOT exercise the decode-branch fail-open logic. It only proves `_scrub` redacts patterns; it does not discriminate the J-001 fix. But the second named test fully discriminates the actual fix.

Let me confirm the full test suite still passes for the file.

All 11 pass. Assessment of the fix itself:

- Appropriate: classifying binary by NUL byte presence and decoding with `errors="replace"` is the correct fail-closed approach. JSONL transcripts with stray non-UTF-8 bytes (latin-1 subprocess dumps, truncated multibyte) now always go through `_scrub` instead of being copied verbatim. This correctly reverses the CV-O-1 fix's fail-open regression. No regression introduced; idiomatic Python. NUL-byte heuristic is the standard "is this binary" test (git uses the same).
- Complete: the named end-to-end test discriminates (fails on revert, passes with fix). The first named test does not discriminate the decode logic but is harmless and asserts a related invariant.

One minor caveat: `errors="replace"` on a multibyte sequence could in theory split a secret across a replacement char, but the patterns here match ASCII credential shapes so this is not a practical gap.

input.reasoning:
Fix verified in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py lines 137-166 (_copy_scrubbed). The fix replaces the pass-6 strict-decode gate (try _scrub(raw.decode("utf-8")) / except UnicodeDecodeError: copy verbatim) with fail-closed logic: classify binary by NUL byte (if b"\x00" in raw: data = raw) else decode with errors="replace" so _scrub ALWAYS runs on text (line 152-155). This correctly reverses the CV-O-1 fail-open re-leak where a single stray non-UTF-8 byte routed the whole transcript to the verbatim branch, bypassing redaction.

APPROPRIATE: Correct and idiomatic. NUL-byte presence is the standard binary-vs-text heuristic (git uses it); errors="replace" guarantees redaction runs on any text containing stray latin-1/truncated-multibyte bytes (common in embedded subprocess stdout). No regression — full tests/test_scripts.py passes (11/11). Minor non-practical caveat: errors='replace' could theoretically split a multibyte-adjacent token, but the credential patterns are ASCII-shaped so secrets are still matched.

COMPLETE (discrimination verified): I reverted the decode branch to the fail-open strict-decode version and re-ran. test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8 FAILED — the secret "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuv" was copied RAW to the destination (assert b"abcdefghijklmnopqrstuv" not in out failed), exactly the re-leak. With the fix it PASSES. So this end-to-end test genuinely discriminates the fix. The other named test (test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes) passed in BOTH states because it calls _scrub directly on pre-decoded text and never exercises the decode/binary-gate branch — it does NOT discriminate the J-001 fix; it only asserts _scrub redacts patterns. The fix is still fully covered by the discriminating end-to-end test. File restored to HEAD after testing (git status clean, errors="replace" present).
